fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility layer for the push service side of things. Basically we keep copy-pasting the same glue code across services and it's causing drift — metrics labels are inconsistent, service URLs get malformed when someone forgets about default ports, and we're sending way too much broadcast traffic because nobody's doing proper delta checks. Can someone build a centralized thing that handles these three pain points?

For the client metadata side, we want to normalize incoming strings into stable labels like we did for the browser detection in that old compatibility module — same idea, 'Other' fallback for anything we don't recognize, but still surface whatever we can parse. OS and browser both need a metrics-safe label AND a human-readable field.

For the service URLs, the router always uses HTTP, the endpoint can vary. The big thing is don't show redundant port numbers — users keep seeing weird URLs with :80 or :443 appended and it looks broken.

For the broadcast delta piece, clients come in with a snapshot of what they have, and we only want to push what changed. New broadcasts shouldn't spam clients who haven't opted in yet. When nothing changed, just say so cleanly.

The whole thing needs a CLI adapter that takes JSON on stdin and spits out line-oriented text so we can black-box test it. Tests should live under rcb_tests/ and a single bash entry point should run everything.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the user-agent piece, the classifier outputs exactly four newline-delimited fields in this order: metrics_os, os, metrics_browser, browser_name. Each field is printed as a key=value line, so the full output for one classification is four lines. On the OS side, only Linux, Windows, and Mac OS X are recognized for the metrics_os field and will produce their normalized name. Anything else should come through as 'Other' for metrics_os, but if we can still tell what the OS was from the user-agent string, the plain os field should keep that parsed value. Same deal for browsers: unrecognized or unsupported browsers use 'Other' for the metrics_browser label, and browser_name should still contain the parsed browser name if one can be extracted from the user-agent string.

Also, on structure, the core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin, stdout, and JSON parsing. The execution adapter is the only layer that handles I/O and JSON. Core domain modules must be independently callable as pure Python (or target-language) functions without any I/O dependency. That applies to the user-agent classification logic described in Feature 1: classify_user_agent as well, specifically the four-field output (metrics_os, os, metrics_browser, browser_name) with 'Other' fallback for unrecognized OSes (only Linux, Windows, Mac OS X are recognized) and browsers.

And one more URL detail since a few people asked the same thing in different ways: the router URL always uses the HTTP scheme. It does not inherit or follow the endpoint scheme. Only the endpoint URL is affected by the endpoint_scheme parameter. For ports, port 80 is omitted for HTTP URLs (both router and endpoint). Port 443 is omitted for HTTPS endpoint URLs. All other non-default ports must be appended as :<port>. So yes, the default port suppression rules in Feature 2 are exactly: port 80 omitted for HTTP URLs, port 443 omitted for HTTPS endpoint URLs, all other ports appended as :<port>.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def classify_user_agent(user_agent: str) -> UserAgentClassification; def build_connection_urls(router_hostname: str, router_port: int, endpoint_scheme: str, endpoint_hostname: str, endpoint_port: int) -> ConnectionUrls; def track_broadcast_changes(initial: list, client: list, actions: list) -> BroadcastDeltaResult

trigger_keywords: what does initial_delta equal when client is up to date
value printed when no changes at initialization
how to express no delta at startup
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the client already holds the correct server version for every broadcast it is subscribed to, initial_delta is printed as 'none' — literally the string 'none', not empty string or null.

trigger_keywords: does adding a new broadcast trigger a delta for existing clients
what happens when a new broadcast is added server-side
update action on a broadcast the client has not subscribed to
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Adding or updating a broadcast on the server side does NOT produce a delta for a client that has not subscribed to that broadcast. The delta is only computed for broadcasts the client is actively subscribed to.

trigger_keywords: what is the format of a broadcast delta value
how are multiple changed broadcasts printed
format for action delta when several broadcasts changed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A single changed broadcast is printed as <id>:<version>. When multiple broadcasts changed in one action, they are comma-separated in the same value, e.g. 'bcasta:rev2,bcastb:rev3'. The key is action_<index>_delta where index is zero-based.

trigger_keywords: what happens when a client subscribes to a broadcast with an old version
subscribe action with outdated version
client subscribes to existing broadcast they are behind on
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a client subscribes to an existing broadcast and their version is outdated compared to the current server version, the delta for that action yields the latest server version for that broadcast ID, formatted as <id>:<latest_version>.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness name output files
test output file naming convention
where are stdout capture files written
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each test case output file is written to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt — for example the first case (index 0) in feature1_user_agent_classification.json run with the default cases dir becomes rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_user_agent_classification@000.txt. The index is zero-padded to three digits.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: clients come in with a snapshot of what they have
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'client' array in the track_broadcast_changes operation — a list of {id, version} objects representing the broadcast versions the client currently holds at connection time

context_pointers.vague_reference: just say so cleanly when nothing changed
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Print 'initial_delta=none' (the literal string 'none') when the client is already up to date with all server broadcast versions

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but I don't have a strong opinion on the technical side — I'd recommend looking at the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the behavior described in the PRD for the specific feature. The test cases are the source of truth for exact output formatting.

feature1_user_agent_classification.json.description: Classify representative user-agent strings into normalized operating-system and browser fields for metrics output.

input.user_agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; U; Linux i686; en-US; rv:1.9.1.2) Gecko/20090807 Mandriva Linux/1.9.1.2-1.1mud2009.1 (2009.1) Firefox/3.5.2 FirePHP/0.3,gzip(gfe),gzip(gfe)

feature2_connection_url_formatting.json.description: Format externally visible router and endpoint base URLs from host, scheme, and port settings while omitting default ports.

feature3_broadcast_change_tracking.json.description: Report only subscribed broadcast identifiers whose server-side versions changed after client initialization, and include new broadcasts only after subscription.
